docs.title: Welcome to Parakram
docs.text: Parakram is an open-source, autonomous hardware compilation engine. It allows developers to deploy full C-based microcontroller firmware and sensor logic through an AI agent directly from the web browser onto real silicon, without compiling native C code.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. The Playground: Provide a plain English prompt summarizing your desired hardware setup (e.g., "turn on the heat lamp when temperature drops below 20C").
  2. The LLM Parser: The Rust backend communicates with Mistral to generate a hardware-validated IR JSON document conforming strictly to Parakram's schemas.
  3. The Bytecode VM: The backend compiles that logic into incredibly fast and secure C Bytecode, routing all dependencies to native HAL drivers configured on the backend.
  4. The Flasher: Use WebSerial to push the payload directly onto an ESP32 via USB natively in your browser!

Start Building ↗

docs.title: Contributing to Parakram
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to the future of zero-code hardware compilation!

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: Parakram has three tiers: 1. Frontend (/playground) — HTML/JS/CSS single-page application 2. Backend (/backend) — Rust (axum) server with Supabase PostgreSQL 3. Firmware (/firmware) — ESP-IDF C project for ESP32-S3

docs.title: Backend
docs.text:
cd backend
cp .env.example .env   # fill in API keys
cargo run

docs.title: Firmware
docs.text:
cd firmware
export IDF_PATH=/path/to/esp-idf
. $IDF_PATH/export.sh
idf.py build

docs.title: Frontend
docs.text: Open http://localhost:8400 when the backend is running.

docs.title: Pull Request Guidelines
docs.text:
• Create a branch: feat/your-feature or fix/your-bug
  • Ensure cargo check and cargo fmt --check pass
  • Add tests for new endpoints
  • Update documentation if adding new features
  • Submit for review

docs.title: Reporting Issues
docs.text: Use the in-app "Report Issue" button, or email varshinicb@vidyuthlabs.co.in.

docs.title: License
docs.text: PolyForm Noncommercial 1.0.0 — see LICENSE for details.

docs.title: AI Pipeline
docs.text: Parakram's AI pipeline converts plain English descriptions into validated, compiled bytecode for microcontrollers.

docs.title: Flow
docs.text:
User Prompt (English)
    ↓ POST /api/llm/intent
LLM (OpenRouter/Mistral/Claude)
    ↓ Structured JSON output
IR Document (validated against schema)
    ↓ POST /api/ir/compile
Bytecode (8-byte instructions, Ed25519 signed)
    ↓ WebSerial / OTA / BLE
ESP32-S3 Firmware VM

docs.title: LLM Integration
docs.text:
The backend supports three LLM providers with automatic fallback:

  1. OpenRouter (preferred) — access to 100+ models via a single API key
  2. Anthropic Claude — direct integration as fallback
  3. Self-hosted — any OpenAI-compatible endpoint via LLM_BASE_URL

docs.title: IR Validation
docs.text:
Every LLM response is validated through an 8-step pipeline (src/ir/validator.rs):

  1. JSON schema conformance
  2. Driver existence verification against the 63-driver registry
  3. Pin conflict detection
  4. Bus compatibility checks
  5. Trigger/action graph validation
  6. Boundary value range checks
  7. Resource limit enforcement
  8. Security policy compliance

docs.title: Quota Enforcement
docs.text: All metered operations (LLM intents, compiles, deploys) are tracked per-user per-month via the billing quota system. Usage resets on the 1st of each month.

docs.title: Bytecode Compiler
docs.text: The Parakram compiler translates validated IR documents into compact, memory-safe bytecode that runs on the ESP32's FreeRTOS virtual machine.

docs.title: Instruction Set Architecture
docs.text:
Every instruction is exactly 8 bytes:

Byte Field Description 0 opcode Operation code (see table below) 1 operand_a First operand (driver index, variable slot, etc.) 2 operand_b Second operand 3 operand_c Third operand 4-7 immediate 32-bit immediate value (i32, f32, or u32)

docs.title: Opcode Categories
docs.text: Range Category Examples 0x00-0x0A Stack ops NOP, LOAD_IMM, STORE_VAR, DUP, POP 0x10-0x1C Arithmetic ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV (integer + float) 0x20-0x29 Comparison EQ, NEQ, GT, LT, AND, OR, NOT 0x40-0x44 Control flow JMP, JMP_IF, JMP_IFNOT, HALT, YIELD 0x60-0x62 Driver I/O DRV_READ, DRV_WRITE, DRV_PWM 0x70-0x72 Comms MQTT_PUB, BLE_NOTIFY, LOG 0x80-0x85 Display/UI DISP_TEXT, DISP_VAL, IMG_BLOB, CREATE_OBJ, SET_PARENT, SET_FLEX 0x90 Timing DELAY_MS 0xF0-0xF1 Pipeline PIPELINE_START, PIPELINE_END

docs.title: Security
docs.text: Every compiled payload includes: - A 4-byte magic header (PRKM) - A 12-byte proprietary watermark (PARAKRAM_NC) - An Ed25519 digital signature for tamper detection - A CRC32 checksum for data integrity

docs.title: Firmware Architecture
docs.text: The Parakram firmware runs on ESP32-S3 microcontrollers using ESP-IDF v5.1 (FreeRTOS).

docs.title: Bytecode Virtual Machine (vm.c)
docs.text: The heart of the firmware. A stack-based interpreter that executes 8-byte instructions at native speed. Zero dynamic allocation in the hot path.

docs.title: Driver Registry (driver_registry.c)
docs.text: Maps bytecode driver indices to physical hardware. Currently supports 63 production drivers covering sensors, actuators, displays, motors, and communication peripherals.

docs.title: LVGL HAL (lvgl_hal.c)
docs.text: Hardware Abstraction Layer bridging the official LVGL 8.3 graphics library to ESP-IDF's SPI display pipeline. Supports ST7789, ILI9341, and SSD1306 displays with DMA-accelerated rendering.

docs.title: Communication Stack
docs.text:
• WiFi (wifi_mgr.c) — Station mode with auto-reconnect
  • BLE (ble_gatt_profile.c) — GATT server for mobile app communication
  • MQTT (mqtt_client.c) — Pub/sub for cloud telemetry

docs.title: OTA Manager (ota_manager.c)
docs.text: Over-the-air firmware updates using ESP-IDF's native esp_ota_ops. SHA-256 verification before committing new partitions.

docs.title: Supported Drivers (63)
docs.text: Category Drivers Temperature DHT22, DS18B20, BME280, BMP280, SHT31, AHT20, MCP9808, HTS221, MLX90614, MAX6675, SI7021 Light BH1750, TSL2561, VEML7700, APDS9960 Motion MPU6050, ADXL345, LIS3DH Gas/Air MQ2, CCS811, SGP30, SCD40, ENS160 Distance HC-SR04, VL53L0X Power INA219 Weight HX711 Water Soil capacitive, pH, Rain, TDS, YF-S201 flow Display SSD1306 OLED, SH1106, ILI9341, ST7789, LCD I2C Touch FT6236, CST816S Motor DC, Stepper, Servo, DRV8833, TB6612 Output Relay, Buzzer, WS2812 LED, Solenoid, MOSFET, Fan PWM Comms NEO-6M GPS Security PIR, Reed switch, MFRC522 RFID Audio INMP441 mic, MAX98357A speaker Camera OV2640

docs.title: WebSerial Flasher
docs.text: Flash compiled Parakram bytecode directly to your ESP32-S3 from your browser. No drivers, no IDE, no Python tools needed.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Google Chrome or Microsoft Edge (Firefox does not support WebSerial)
  • An ESP32-S3 development board connected via USB
  • Compiled bytecode from the Playground

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. Generate firmware in the Playground — type your hardware logic in plain English, review the IR, and compile.
  2. Navigate to the WebFlasher — click "WebFlasher" in the navigation bar or go to /flasher.html.
  3. Connect your device — click "Connect Target Device". Chrome will prompt you to select the USB serial port.
  4. Flash — click "Flash Compiled Bytecode". The flasher streams the binary payload in 256-byte chunks over the serial connection at 115200 baud.
  5. Done — the device reboots into your new firmware automatically.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text: Problem Solution "WebSerial API not supported" Use Chrome or Edge. Firefox/Safari don't support it. No device appears in popup Check USB cable, install CP2102/CH340 drivers if needed. "No compiled bytecode found" Go back to the Playground and generate + compile first. Write fails mid-stream Reduce baud rate or check USB cable quality.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Rust (stable toolchain)
  • A free Supabase project (PostgreSQL database)
  • An OpenRouter API key (for LLM compilation)

docs.title: 1. Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/YourOrg/Parakram.git
cd Parakram

docs.title: 2. Configure Environment
docs.text:
cd backend
cp .env.example .env

Edit .env with your credentials: 

SUPABASE_DB_URL=postgres://postgres:yourpassword@db.yourproject.supabase.co:5432/postgres
SUPABASE_URL=https://yourproject.supabase.co
JWT_SECRET=your-supabase-jwt-secret
OPENROUTER_API_KEY=sk-or-v1-your-key
STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=sk_test_your-stripe-key
STRIPE_PRICE_MAKER=price_your-stripe-price-id
SENDGRID_API_KEY=SG.your-sendgrid-key

docs.title: 3. Run the Backend
docs.text:
cargo run
The server starts on http://localhost:8400.

docs.title: 4. Open the Playground
docs.text: Navigate to http://localhost:8400 in Chrome or Edge. The playground UI loads automatically.
